explanations.1: Unlike most skeletal muscles that pull on bones, the muscles of facial expression have their insertions and origins in the skin to create movement of the face.
explanations.2: A synergist assists the agonist, or prime mover, in performing its action, while an antagonist provides the opposite movement.
explanations.3: Flexion is a movement that decreases the angle between bones at a joint, such as bending the elbow or knee.
explanations.4: The origin is the end of the muscle attached to a fixed or stabilized bone, which remains relatively stationary during contraction.
explanations.5: Pectoralis major features a convergent pattern where fascicles spread over a large area and converge at a single, common attachment point.
explanations.6: Parallel muscles have fascicles that are arranged in the same direction as the long axis of the muscle, like the biceps brachii.
explanations.7: In a bipennate arrangement, the muscle fibers feed into a long central tendon from both sides, similar to the structure of a feather.
explanations.8: The specific attachment points of a muscle's origin and insertion determine the direction of the pull and the resulting action on the skeleton.
explanations.9: The temporalis muscle is named for its location on the side of the head, covering the temporal bone of the skull.
explanations.10: Muscle names usually combine logic regarding size and action; 'minimus' (smallest) and 'longus' (long) used together is contradictory and not standard naming.
explanations.11: An abductor is a muscle that moves a limb away from the midline of the body, whereas an adductor moves it toward the midline.
explanations.12: The sternocleidomastoid muscles are the prime movers for head flexion when both sides contract simultaneously.
explanations.13: The inferior oblique is one of the extrinsic eye muscles located in the orbit (eye socket) responsible for eye movement.
explanations.14: The masseter is the main muscle of mastication (chewing) that elevates the mandible to close the mouth.
explanations.15: The suffix '-glossus' is derived from the Greek word for tongue, and is used to name the extrinsic muscles that move the tongue.
explanations.16: The erector spinae group provides postural support and is the primary muscle mass for extending the vertebral column.
explanations.17: The quadratus lumborum is part of the posterior abdominal wall, while the rectus abdominis and obliques form the anterior abdominal wall.
explanations.18: The diaphragm is the primary muscle for breathing, and the scalene muscles assist by elevating the ribs during inhalation.
explanations.19: The linea alba is a white, fibrous band of connective tissue that runs vertically down the center of the abdomen.
explanations.20: The trapezius is the large, superficial muscle of the upper back that covers the deeper rhomboid major and minor muscles.
explanations.21: The triceps brachii is the prime mover (agonist) for extending the forearm at the elbow joint.
explanations.22: The medial epicondyle of the humerus serves as the common origin for the muscles that flex the wrist and fingers.
explanations.23: Both axial muscles (originating on the trunk) and appendicular muscles (associated with limbs) cooperate to stabilize the pectoral girdle.
explanations.24: The gluteal muscle group, particularly the gluteus maximus, attaches the leg to the pelvis and is the main extensor of the hip.
explanations.25: The sartorius is the longest muscle in the body and performs the combined actions of flexion, abduction, and lateral rotation needed to cross the legs.
explanations.26: The gastrocnemius is the large, superficial muscle that forms the bulk of the calf in the lower leg.
explanations.27: The vastus intermedius is one of the four quadriceps muscles and is located directly deep to the rectus femoris.
